Pirouette — four-subject comparison

AdrienLauraLeonieManon · 21 valid takes  ·  Motive @ 360 Hz  ·  Per guide §6.4 (whole-body rotation, in-place): rotation span detected dynamically
Four pirouettes, one turn each. All four subjects complete approximately one full rotation per take (1.11 / 1.04 / 1.01 / 1.08 turns for Adrien / Laura / Leonie / Manon respectively). The differentiating factors are how they spin: rotation speed, axis verticality, free-foot lift, and horizontal drift. Each subject has a distinct signature on a different combination of these axes. The most informative finding is in the dynamics: two of the four subjects' body-axis tilt rises during the rotation rather than staying flat (see Fig 2) — they progressively destabilize as the spin develops, which the mid-50% summary alone hides.

1. Angular velocity profile

2. Body-axis tilt during rotation

3. Hip drift during rotation

4. Foot pattern (support / free leg)

5. Within-subject consistency

6. Summary metrics

Five-six takes per subject, median ± std. Cells tinted: green = better, red = worse. Duration is neutral (faster ≠ better — depends on technique). Turns CV is the within-subject coefficient of variation on number of turns: lower = more reproducible rotation amount.

7. Key observations

  • Turns — typical: Adrien highest (1.11 ± 0.10) — Leonie lowest (1.01 ± 0.04)
  • Turns — best: Adrien highest (1.16) — Leonie lowest (1.04)
  • Peak ω (°/s): Manon highest (557 ± 47) — Leonie lowest (424 ± 17)
  • Hip drift (cm): Laura lowest (15.7 ± 8.5) — Leonie highest (47.2 ± 11.7)
  • Body tilt mid-50% (°): Manon lowest (4.9° ± 3.2) — Adrien highest (10.7° ± 1.1)
  • Turns CV: Leonie lowest (3.8%) — Adrien highest (9.2%)
Synthesis. Three findings. (i) Quantity of rotation is uniform: all four subjects produce ~1 turn (range 0.93-1.16 across takes), so the turns metric doesn't discriminate. The signature lives in how they spin. (ii) Each subject has a distinct signature on a different combination of axes — there's no single ranking. Manon = fastest peak (557°/s), most vertical axis (4.9°), lowest free-foot lift (21 cm). Leonie = slowest (424°/s), most consistent in turn count (CV 4%), highest free-foot lift (71 cm — pronounced retiré), but largest horizontal drift (47 cm). Laura = second fastest (535°/s), smallest drift (16 cm), mid-range lift. Adrien = moderate speed (468°/s), small drift (17 cm), but largest tilt (10.7°) and an unusual U-shaped tilt profile (see Fig 2). (iii) Tilt dynamics during the rotation are diagnostic, not just the mid-50% summary. Manon and Laura keep their tilt roughly flat across the rotation (around 4-9°) — controlled axis throughout. Adrien starts upright, plunges to 4° at 25%, then rises back to 13° through the mid-50% — his entry is clean but he progressively destabilizes during the spin. Leonie starts at 3° and tilts up to 11° through the mid-50% — active destabilization in the same direction as her drift. The mid-50% mean alone hides this: a coach could use Adrien's and Leonie's profiles to identify when in the spin the technique breaks down. Rotation-direction preferences are stable per subject: Adrien and Manon spin clockwise on every take, Laura and Leonie counter-clockwise.
Limits of this analysis. Limits of this analysis. n = 1 subject per identity. The rotation amount metric (turns) is uniform across all subjects (~1 turn) so the comparison is on quality, not quantity. Adrien's source files include a 'Raquette' rigid body in the Motive scene — this is a leftover object from another session unrelated to the pirouette gesture and is not used in the analysis.

Data-quality probe: worst NaN coverage in any rotation span = 0.00% on (longest gap 0 frames). No takes were rejected. Hip angular velocity is computed from the Hip quaternion derivative; for cleaner pirouettes the rotation axis is essentially vertical (Y) and ω_y captures it well, but for subjects with a tilted axis the projection onto Y underestimates the true rotation rate by a factor cos(tilt). All four subjects had tilts ≤ 11° so the projection error is < 2% — negligible for this comparison but worth noting for tilted spins.

Direction sign was normalized per take so all rotations appear positive (CCW convention) — see guide §6.4 for the rationale.
Pipeline: mocap.py + report.py from the project knowledge base. Family: §6.4 whole-body rotation (in-place variant). POI = onset of rotation (|ω_y| > 60°/s sustained). ROI = the rotation span itself. Vertical Y, quaternions (x, y, z, w). Smoothing: Savitzky-Golay window 21, polynomial 3.
